sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 116, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Player (green, 2x3) at rows 41-43, cols 32-35 - Three blue squares (2x2 each) in dark gray frames (3x3 each) arranged in a triangle - Blue square 3 frame blocks upward path (rows 36-39, cols 32-35) - UP movement moves player up 4 rows when clear; blocked when obstacle in path - Goal unclear - likely navigate to collect/interact with blue squares
- Plan: Navigate to remaining blue squares and press SPACE to remove their frames.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 115 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the early transitions:
1. Initial: Player at rows 49-51, cols 32-35; White at row 48, cols 32-35; 3 c frames at rows 24-27/44-47, 28-31/16-19, 36-39/32-35
2. After UP: Player moved to rows 45-47, white to row 44
3. After UP: Player moved to rows 41-43, white to row 40; bottom c frame disappeared (replaced by HUD bar at row 63)
4. After UP: Player stayed at rows 41-43 (blocked by bottom frame at rows 36-39)
5. After LEFT: Player moved to rows 40-43, cols 28-29; white became 4x4 at rows 40-43, col 28
6. After UP: Player moved to rows 37-39, cols 28; white at row 36, cols 28-31
So the player moved UP through the bottom frame area (rows 36-39, cols 32-35), and the frame disappeared. But the player was at cols 28-29, not 32-35. So the player didn't move THROUGH the frame, but the frame disappeared anyway.
Wait, looking more carefully at transition 1: the c frame at [36, 32] disappeared and was replaced by a c at [63, 63] (the HUD bar). So the bottom frame was "collected" when the player moved UP, even though the player was at cols 32-35 and the frame was at cols 32-35.
But in transition 3 (LEFT), the player moved to cols 28-29, and the c frame at [36, 32] reappeared! So the frame reappeared when the player moved LEFT.
This is very strange. The frame disappears when the player is at the same columns and moves UP, but reappears when the player moves away.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the c frames are "shadows" or "projections" of the player's position. When the player is at a certain column range, a c frame appears below. When the player moves away, the frame reappears at its original position.
Actually, looking at the current state:
- Player at rows 21-23, cols 28-31
- c frame at rows 28-31, cols 32-35
- c frame at rows 32-35, cols 28-31
- c frame at rows 32-35, cols 12-15
The c frames seem to be "below" the player. The player is at rows 21-23, and the frames are at rows 28-31 and 32-35.
Wait, I think I understand now. The c frames are "dropping" down as the player moves UP. Each time the player moves UP, the frames shift down. The bottom frame was at rows 36-39 initially, and now the frames are at rows 28-31 and 32-35.
But this doesn't explain why the frames are at different columns. The initial frames were at cols 44-47, 16-19, and 32-35. The current frames are at cols 32-35, 28-31, and 12-15.
Hmm, the columns have shifted left by 4 each time the player moved LEFT. The player moved LEFT once (from cols 32-35 to cols 28-29), and the frames shifted left.
Actually, I think the frames are "attached" to the player's column position. When the player is at cols 32-35, a frame appears at cols 32-35. When the player moves LEFT to cols 28-29, the frame shifts to cols 28-31.
But there are 3 frames, not 1. So maybe each frame corresponds to a different "layer" or "depth" below the player.
OK, I'm overcomplicating this. Let me try a different approach: what if the goal is simply to move the player to the top of the screen? The player started at rows 49-51 and has moved UP to rows 21-23. Maybe the goal is to reach row 0 or some top position.
Let me try moving UP more to see if the player can reach the top.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 21-23, cols 28-31
# Try moving UP multiple times to reach the top
# Each UP moves 4 rows up
result = action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP'])
print(f"After 5x UP: gameplay={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'G', 'c', 'b', 'g']:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
print(f"level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
